summary:
The Terramare culture was a Bronze Age civilization of the Po Valley in northern Italy, flourishing approximately 1700–1150 BC. The name derives from the Italian dialectal term for the dark, fertile earth found at their settlement sites. Terramare settlements were distinctive: they were built on wooden piles above low-lying terrain, laid out in a roughly rectangular grid plan, surrounded by earthen embankments and defensive ditches, and oriented with precise attention to cardinal directions. This degree of urban planning is remarkable for Bronze Age Europe and suggests a sophisticated level of communal organization.

Terramare communities practiced a mixed economy of agriculture, pastoralism, and craft production, including significant bronze working. Their settlements were organized around an internal street grid and show evidence of standardized house construction, suggesting a degree of communal oversight or regulation. At their height, Terramare communities in the Po Valley formed a dense network of interacting settlements that may have constituted a relatively integrated regional society.

Around 1150 BC the Terramare culture underwent a rapid and severe collapse. Settlements were abandoned, and the population appears to have dispersed. The causes remain debated but likely include a combination of environmental stress — possibly related to the same broader climatic disruptions affecting the [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]] — and over-exploitation of local agricultural resources. The collapse of Terramare culture preceded and may have contributed to the demographic upheaval in the Italian peninsula that preceded the emergence of the Iron Age Italic cultures, including the ancestors of the Romans.
